messages.subject: Contributing to The Phage?
messages.snippet: Hey Evan, I got your email from Olya who has co-organized talks with Phage in the past. My girlfriend Jaybird (cc'd) and I are very curious about camping with Phage! We hung out a lot at your camp last year for science talks, ask a drunk sc
messages.text:
Hey Evan,
I got your email from Olya who has co-organized talks with Phage in the
past.
My girlfriend Jaybird (cc'd) and I are very curious about camping with
Phage! We hung out a lot at your camp last year for science talks, ask a
drunk scientist, etc.
I'm a mechanical engineer and my girlfriend is a chemist and materials
engineer - we would love to contribute to the nerdy culture 🤓
This will be my second year at burning man. My girlfriend is a veteran
burner, has built big art (ex flaming lotus girls), and has volunteered
with the org (dpw power and burners without boarders).
We would love to learn more about how we can contribute!
All the best,
Samstorm

messages.subject: 🚀 BFC AI Gateway is Recruiting Interns
messages.snippet: View this post on the web at https://bayareafoundersclub.substack.com/p/bfc-ai-gateway-is-recruiting-interns Are you passionate about AI infrastructure, LLMs, AI agents, and model optimization? Want to work on a real AI product that’s solvi
messages.text:
View this post on the web at https://bayareafoundersclub.substack.com/p/bfc-ai-gateway-is-recruiting-interns
Are you passionate about AI infrastructure, LLMs, AI agents, and model optimization? Want to work on a real AI product that’s solving meaningful problems instead of building another demo?
Join BFC AI Gateway.
We’re building a simpler, smarter way for developers and AI teams to use foundation models.
Instead of manually choosing models, users simply provide:
✅ Use Case
✅ Agent Workflow
✅ Monthly Budget
✅ Quality Requirement (Minimum Pass Rate)
Our system automatically recommends:
🎯 The optimal weighted combination of AI models
💰 Monthly API cost projections
📉 Estimated cost savings
📚 Evidence-backed explanations for every routing decision
Our mission is simple:
Transform AI model selection from experience-driven to data-driven.
We’re actively collaborating with AI researchers, startup founders, and foundation model teams to advance Smart Routing—helping every AI request find the best balance between quality, cost, and latency.
What You’ll Work On
Depending on your interests and background, you may contribute to:
AI Agent architecture and workflow design
LLM benchmarking and model evaluation
Smart Routing algorithms and optimization
Prompt engineering and evaluation pipelines
Full-stack AI product development
Developer Relations and technical content
Product operations and user growth
We’re Looking For People Who Want To
Work alongside AI researchers, founders, and engineers in Silicon Valley
Stay at the forefront of the latest advances in large language models and AI agents
Help build an AI infrastructure product from 0 to 1
Gain hands-on experience shipping real products used by customers—not just prototypes
Whether you’re a student, researcher, engineer, or aspiring founder, if you’re excited about building the future of AI infrastructure, we’d love to hear from you.
Location
📍 Silicon Valley (Hybrid; some roles can be fully remote)
Apply
📩 Email your resume and a brief introduction to:
paul@bayareafoundersclub.com [ mailto:paul@bayareafoundersclub.com ]
Come build the next generation of AI infrastructure with us—and help create one of the world’s leading AI Gateways.
About the Bay Area Founders Club’s President
About Bay Area Founders Club
Bay Area Founders Club (BFC), founded by Dr. Paul Fang [ https://substack.com/redirect/2056b120-6d5f-4d79-990d-a12d1a8ea0bb?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ] in Silicon Va

messages.subject: Oak Trees Protecting Our Shoreline in Alameda are in Peril!
messages.to: Carolyn Mason <lpemail62@gmail.com>, Marla Koss ABG <marlakoss@yahoo.com>, Alison Limoges <aslimoges@gmail.com>, Cindy Lacroix <cjlacroix@aol.com>, Birgitt Evans <reachbirgitt@gmail.com>, Tokuda, Jasmine <Jasmine@jasminebazaar.com>
messages.snippet: Good afternoon folks, I've written up the text of my statement to Alameda City Council (See below) and I plan to send a copy via email to each City Council member, so that they have a public record of it. You are welcome to copy my text, ed
messages.text:
Good afternoon folks,
I've written up the text of my statement to Alameda City Council (See
below) and I plan to send a copy via email to each City Council member, so
that they have a public record of it. You are welcome to copy my text, edit
it to your preference, write your own statement or take no action as you
see fit.
Being that I was the main member of CASA advocating for Living Shorelines
and nature-based adaptation to sea-level rise, in our Climate Action
Resilience Plan. I feel implementing the plan in a way that is most likely
to succeed and respects the nature-based adaptation already present and
helping to stabilize the shoreline is important. I realize that my passion
on this subject may not be felt by everyone, and I respect that.
Here's a helpful guide on how to write an email to every City Council
Member.
https://alamedacitizenstaskforce.org/get-involved/how-to-write-to-city-officials/
City Meeting Tues July 21, 2026
From: Damian Mason, member of CASA, OAAC, Alameda Backyard Growers (ABG)
To: Members of Alameda City Council
I’m glad to hear that we may soon have the funds to upgrade our storm drain
infrastructure, implement nature-based shoreline protections and more.  CA
native oak trees of 10 inches or more in diameter are protected in Alameda,
Oakland and other cities in CA.
I’m here on behalf of a small number of young Coastal Live Oaks growing
along the Harbor Bay Shoreline trail between Sea View Park and Harbor Bay
Club. Coastal live oaks stabilize shorelines with deep roots, reducing
erosion. Studies show they outperform hard structures like seawalls,
promoting natural resilience.  This is a key part of nature’s adaptation to
sea level rise. The oak trees in question are only a few years old and 1-3
inch diameter. Unfortunately, the current plan is to remove all of them.
Given their size, they are not yet protected trees, but every small acorn
has the potential to quickly grow into a mighty majestic oak.  I’m
confident we can find a way to incorporate them into the city's
nature-based sea-level rise adaptation plan.
I’ve personally witnessed the dedication Alameda’s city staff and officials
have shown to building Climate Resilience through applied nature-based
design. I trust a path can be found within the shoreline designs to
preserve these trees by incorporating them into the final design plan.
Thank you for your time and attention on this matter.
Damian Mason,
Regenerate, Restore, and Rewild our Ecosystems  #OurCityOurFuture
CASA

messages.snippet: Serverless inference vs. GPU performance tutorial‌ ‌ ‌ Grok Build increases concerns around code security #outlook a { padding:0; } table { border-spacing:0;} img { border:0;height:auto;line-height:100%; outline:none;text-decoration:none;-m
messages.text: Serverless inference vs. GPU performance tutorial‌ ‌ ‌ Grok Build increases concerns around code security #outlook a { padding:0; } table { border-spacing:0;} img { border:0;height:auto;line-height:100%; outline:none;text-decoration:none;-ms-interpolation-mode:bicubic; } p { display:block; } 96 table { border-collapse:collapse;mso-table-lspace:0pt;mso-table-rspace:0pt; } .mj-outlook-group-fix { width:100% !important; } @import url(https://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Roboto:400,400i,700,700i); @media only screen and (min-width:480px) { .mj-column-per-100 { width:100% !important; max-width: 100%; } } body, .body { margin:0;padding:0;-webkit-text-size-adjust:100%;-ms-text-size-adjust:100%; } .links-0069FF-underline a { color: #0069FF; text-decoration: underline; } .links-FFFFFF-underline a { color: #FFFFFF; text-decoration: underline; } ul{display: block;}sup, sub{line-height:0;}body a{text-decoration: none; color: #0080FF;}@media only screen and (min-width: 480px) { .hide-on-mobile{display:block !important;}.hide-on-desktop{display:none !important;} }.hide-on-desktop{display:block;}.hide-on-mobile{display:none;} Serverless inference vs. GPU performance tutorial ICYMI AI: The latest AI trends, delivered to your inbox Grok Build: When "Private" Is a Setting, Not a Guarantee The hardest part of adopting AI coding agents in 2026 isn't getting one to understand your codebase. That part is almost too easy. Point Grok, Claude Code, or Codex at a repo, and it reads the whole thing, opens a terminal, and starts shipping commits all in quick succession. No, the hard part is knowing where that potentially sensitive data goes once the agent is done reading it: which server it's cached on, which bucket it's staged in, and on which infrastructure your source code now lives. A recent Grok Build piece from The Hacker News dives into the details, and the reality is not reassuring. Security researchers found xAI's Grok Build CLI was bundling developers' entire Git history, including commits, comments, unredacted API keys, and database passwords, into a Git bundle. This information was then shipped to a Google Cloud bucket, at roughly 27,800 times the data volume the coding task actually required. Even more concerning? Flipping the tool's own privacy toggle did nothing; the server kept collecting this data regardless of the client-side setting. messages.subject: Nobel Laureate James Rothman on the Future of Nanobiology | September 8
messages.snippet: Dear Jay, The future of medicine is being engineered at the molecular scale. From targeted drug delivery and bioprinted tissues to precision therapies, today's biggest breakthroughs in healthcare are being driven by nanobiology. But none of
messages.text:
Dear Jay,
The future of medicine is being engineered at the molecular scale.
From targeted drug delivery and bioprinted tissues to precision therapies, today's biggest breakthroughs in healthcare are being driven by nanobiology. But none of this would be possible without understanding one of biology's most fundamental processes: how cells transport and deliver molecules.
Join SOSV Deep Tech LIVE for an exclusive fireside chat with Professor James Rothman (https://medicine.yale.edu/profile/james-rothman/?utm_source=SOSV&utm_campaign=be42e78c68-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2026_01_12_09_38_COPY_01&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-7b8ca69b84-294129372) , recipient of the 2013 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine and Sterling Professor of Cell Biology at Yale University.
* Register In-Person (https://luma.com/gab9v17y?utm_source=SOSV&utm_campaign=be42e78c68-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2026_01_12_09_38_COPY_01&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-7b8ca69b84-294129372) (New York)
* Register Virtual (https://luma.com/0q7y58no?utm_source=SOSV&utm_campaign=be42e78c68-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2026_01_12_09_38_COPY_01&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-7b8ca69b84-294129372)  (LinkedIn Live Streaming)
Originally trained as a physicist before becoming a biochemist, Professor Rothman's pioneering discoveries uncovered the molecular machinery that controls transport within cells. His work transformed modern cell biology, laid the foundation for countless biotechnology innovations, and continues to shape the future of precision medicine.
Beyond academia, Professor Rothman has spent decades bringing breakthrough science to market. He co-founded ARIAD Pharmaceuticals, served as Chief Scientist at GE Healthcare, and advises leading biotech startups and venture firms, including Prellis Biologics, Celesta Capital, and Arsenal Capital Partners. His career is a rare example of the journey from breakthrough research to commercial impact.
Moderated by SOSV Managing Partner Sean O'Sullivan (https://sosv.com/team/sean-osullivan/?utm_source=SOSV&utm_campaign=be42e78c68-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2026_01_12_09_38_COPY_01&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-7b8ca69b84-294129372) , the conversation will explore:
* Why nanobiology could redefine the future of medicine
* How physics unlocked the secrets of living cells
* The path from breakthrough science to successful biotech companies
* Bioprinting, synthetic biology, and the next generation of therapeutics
* What deep tech investors look for when backing scientific founders

messages.subject: this week at tiat (7/20 - 7/26)
messages.snippet: this week at tiat (7/20 - 7/26) tasteslop, sex slop, post slop, signal slop, and more ... It’s the last week of the Epistemologies of Slop, our group exhibition exploring how meaning, trust, taste, and culture get made under conditions of a
messages.text:
this week at tiat (7/20 - 7/26)
tasteslop, sex slop, post slop, signal slop, and more ... It’s the last week of the Epistemologies of Slop, our group exhibition exploring how meaning, trust, taste, and culture get made under conditions of algorithmic and AI excess. this week’s events ✦ Monday, July 20th 7-8pm - Physical Fictions | Speculative Everything book club | week six of our Speculative Everything reading group. this week is a special workshop facilitated with Toshi Anders Hoo on speculative design in practice, physical fictions, and creating artifacts from possible futures. Physical Fictions: Invitations to Make Believe | Speculative Everything book club - https://luma.com/event/evt-G3BYdKHPvGrkHFW Tuesday, July 21st 7-9pm - Let’s Play FUCKSWEEPER | a live performance and playthrough of a Minesweeper-fetish dating sim about the slopification of sex, followed by a conversation with the game’s co-creators on slop, art, humanity, risk, and video games. ∞ Let's Play FUCKSWEEPER: a video game about the slopification of sex - https://luma.com/event/evt-WmLE0ugIe7Mzydu Wednesday, July 22nd 7-8pm - Beyond Slop | artist, toolmaker, and Artbreeder creator Joel Simon joins Feileacan Kirkbride McCormick for a conversation on creative AI, process-based tools, artistic voice, and what might come after prompt-based slop. ∞ Beyond Slop - https://luma.com/event/evt-jUSinEH3oKgsOVV Thursday, July 23rd 7-8pm - Signal, Noise, Slop | a conversation with Andrew McLuhan and Ruby Justice Thelot on algorithmic ecology, media theory, cultural practice, digital phenomenology, and the conditions that produce slop. ∞ A conversation with Andrew McLuhan & Ruby Justice Thelot | on Signal, Noise, Slop - https://luma.com/event/evt-6otxJMcH9XBKl9z Friday, July 24th 7-8:30pm - tasteslop w/ Emily Segal | Emily Segal joins us for a lecture on “tasteslop”, what it is, what it means, and what is next. ∞ tasteslop w/ Emily Segal - https://luma.com/event/evt-u52TP2m0s2uKwkg Saturday, July 25th 3-4pm - the Epistemologies of Slop closing reception | come through for the final gathering of the exhibition. new artworks have been added, new artworks and live performances, and it’s your last chance to inhabit the slopocene with us. ∞ the Epistemologies of Slop | Art + Tech Exhibition Closing Reception - https://luma.com/event/evt-Vse611kGhw8CdrP coming soon ✦ Monday, July 27th 7-8pm - Aesthetics of Unreality | Speculative Everything book club | week seven of the reading group continues. Aesthetic

messages.text:
What’s happening in San Francisco Week of July 20
Fog rolls in as usual this week, with highs in the 60s and 70s, but the streets get busy Saturday when the SF Marathon closes major roads across town. Stern Grove's free concert series continues Sunday, and the Jewish Film Festival keeps screening through August 2.
SoMa and the Mission have a full slate of founder meetups and infra talks this week, with YC's AI Startup School drawing extra crowds and afterparties. If you'd rather unwind, Friday's Bhangra & Beats Night Market brings music and food trucks to Yerba Buena Gardens.

messages.subject: Bay Area Events For The Week Of July 20
messages.snippet: View this post on the web at https://bayareafoundersclub.substack.com/p/bay-area-events-for-the-week-of-july-ea3 Hi BFC Fam ~ Welcome back to this week’s BFC Bay Area roundup. This week features a dynamic mix of AI, startup, and builder-foc
messages.text:
View this post on the web at https://bayareafoundersclub.substack.com/p/bay-area-events-for-the-week-of-july-ea3
Hi BFC Fam ~
Welcome back to this week’s BFC Bay Area roundup.
This week features a dynamic mix of AI, startup, and builder-focused events across the Bay Area — from hackathons and technical workshops to founder meetups, demo nights, and community gatherings.
Some highlights include OpenAI Build Week Community Meetup - San Francisco, Omnigent & Multi-Agent Coding Meetup @ Databricks SF, OpenAI Builder Lounge SF with Parallel, and Agentic Builders Night, bringing together builders and innovators exploring the latest in AI development, agents, and emerging technologies.
Founders and operators can connect at events like Foundry Live Demo Night, YC Reject Pitch Night, Dwarkesh Unplugged, and AI Engineering Leaders Dinner/Networking/Talks, while community-focused gatherings like SF Intern & New Grad Summerfest, Build Fast. Launch Loud. Prompt Driven Hackathon, and YC AI Startup School Afterparty offer opportunities to meet the next generation of builders.
Let’s get into this week’s events. 🚀
This Week in the Bay Area: 45 Curated Events
Featured Event
🎟 How to Raise Venture Capital — A Workshop with Legendary Silicon Valley Investor Miko Matsumura [ https://substack.com/redirect/df413fd7-6f7a-4f89-8340-b14a125a5578?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ]
After an overwhelmingly positive response to our first session, we’re excited to welcome Miko Matsumura back for another fundraising masterclass on August 13.
With over 25 years of experience as a founder, operator, and investor, Miko will share practical insights into how VCs evaluate startups, make investment decisions, and what founders can do to improve their chances of getting funded.
During this interactive session, you’ll learn:
💡 What makes a startup venture-backable
📈 How VCs evaluate founders, markets, and opportunities
🤝 How to build investor relationships before fundraising
❌ Common fundraising and pitching mistakes
🏛️ What actually happens inside a VC partnership meeting
💬 Live Q&A with Miko
Whether you’re raising a Pre-Seed, Seed, or Series A, this workshop will help you approach fundraising with greater confidence and clarity.
🎟 Spots are limited. Register HERE! [ https://substack.com/redirect/df413fd7-6f7a-4f89-8340-b14a125a5578?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ]
Monday, July 20
1. Cafe Cursor San Francisco: Startup E

Welcome to the weekend.
Did your brilliant friend forward this to you? Subscribe below.
Prime Numbers
216 billion — The world consumes 216 billion [ https://substack.com/redirect/b7b917fa-ed89-48b8-a0a5-499a2dff185e?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ] pounds of bananas annually (12 kilograms per person), but Fusarium wilt fungus and climate change threaten the dominant Cavendish cultivar, which could see suitable growing land in Latin America shrink 60 percent by 2080.
17.9 — Despite accounting for fewer total goals, headers that successfully assist plays are becoming more accurate. 17.9 percent [ https://substack.com/redirect/10040cb3-8979-4b83-9ca4-bcbb2f8b5b52?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ] of World Cup goals came from headers through the round of 16 this year, down from 23.7 percent in 2018, suggesting players are getting more disciplined with this technique.
10 — China’s half-century effort to fight desert expansion through the “Green Great Wall” program has worked. Desertified land has decreased by 10 percent [ https://substack.com/redirect/4f4dd14d-af15-4545-89d1-93b013c5f2c9?j=eyJ1IjoiN2EzYzFhIn0.gmSjAtdlofMqOFUjgfQgnPyMBlCD_j00mTQEinip6mM ] overall, with forest cover in the program area rising from 5 percent in 1978 to 14 percent in 2022.
Act Now on AI
More than 200 economists, AI researchers, and tech executives signed an open letter warning that artificial intelligence could drive “an unprecedented transformation of our economy, larger than the Industrial Revolution” over the next decade. The risks include large-scale job displacement, but the signatories—including 16 Nobel Prize winners—argue institutions have a choice. AI researcher Yoshua Bengio emphasized that the transformation is “highly plausible,” and urged collective action. “We must be intentional and make collective, democratic choices, rather than letting market forces play out and risking leaving most citizens behind.” The letter calls on leaders to build guardrails and institutions that steer AI toward complementing humans and benefiting society. messages.text:
Where there are fewer applicants, you've got better odds.
*5 regions sitters rarely visit*
On Nomador, some regions in France are less popular than Brittany or Provence. Not because they're less beautiful. Just because fewer people talk about them.
Five current examples:
🏞️ *Aveyron* : stone houses, shepherd dogs, lavender in summer
🌄 *Cantal* : cottages with cats, hikes right outside the door
🏡 *Tarn-et-Garonne* : large family homes, several pets
🌌 *Lozère* : silence, stars, animals roaming free
🏰 *Berry* : castles, novels to finish, cats on the windowsills
For applications, these regions have less competition. For your first sit, that's where to look.

Dear Gardyner,
You've probably seen the headlines by now: a Cyclospora outbreak
<https://mygardyn.com/blog/2026-cyclospora-outbreak/> likely tied to lettuce
has sickened close to 7,000 people across 34 states.
It's not the first time, either. Spinach one year, romaine the next, basil
after that. The names change, but the pattern doesn't: food changes hands
dozens of times and travels thousands of miles before it lands on your plate.
Somewhere in that chain, things can go wrong.
We built Gardyn to get around that chain entirely.
Your Gardyn produce is harvested minutes before you eat it, in your own
kitchen. No truck, no warehouse, no unknown hands. Just you, picking what
you're about to serve your family. There’s relief in knowing you can stop
scanning recall lists – you can trust what you grow.
That's peace of mind worth passing along. If the people you care about are
starting to think harder about where their food comes from, it’s the perfect
time to share your referral code. Give $100, get $100 — they’ll be one step
